column4: Sjölund, Leijon, and Eriksson 2020 Table II; source ranges accessed from the e-magnetsuk online ferrite-grades page on 2019-09-25 and represented by the authors as midpoint plus or minus half-range for their finite-element model
column5: The paper prints Br 385 plus or minus 15 mT, Hcb 192.5 plus or minus 17.5 kA/m, Hcj 200 plus or minus 20 kA/m, and BHmax 28 plus or minus 2 kJ/m3, then states that the average values are used for every magnitude. The runtime claims retain only those four source-used midpoints because the printed half-ranges are catalog bounds without statistical confidence semantics. The online source identifies a grade family, not a purchased specimen: physical product, manufacturer, supplier of a product, composition, production lot, sinter schedule, magnetization state, magnetic test method, and property test temperature are absent. The paper runs its simulation at 20 degC, but that model temperature is not laundered into a material measurement temperature or service-temperature qualification

column4: Sjölund, Leijon, and Eriksson 2020 Equation 2 evaluated at the Table II Y30 remanence and maximum-energy-product midpoints
column5: The source derives mu_r = Br squared divided by four times mu_0 times BHmax after assuming a linear second-quadrant B-H branch above the maximum-energy-product point, then assumes isotropic constant permeability because transverse data are absent. The resulting 1.0531580999909012 is a finite-element model input derived from two catalog midpoints. It is not measured recoil permeability, a minor-loop slope, a temperature-dependent recoil law, or an irreversible demagnetization boundary. The authors explicitly report that no adequate Y30 demagnetization curve was found to validate the modeled knee point
